raw_payload.author: Lookypooky123
raw_payload.content: I will make this quick. With all the hype I was super excited to see this movie, especially since I like Uncut Gems. I can sum this movie up for you spoiler free - its Uncut Gems without the stakes or character arcs. This movie is very well shot, acted, and put together. However, the script and the flow of the story is what takes it down. By the end Marty has seemed to learn nothing and have very little consequences for his actions - although there is a forced scene at the end that says "see look, he is changed now!". I dont believe it. I would recommend since its a good time, but its not gonna blow you away and become your next favorite movie.

raw_payload.author: gwarren-63-539064
raw_payload.content: Timothée Chalamet is a solid actor and his character is fun to watch in this film. But I kept wondering if I'm supposed to like him. He's a self-centered jerk who doesn't seem to learn from his mistakes and doesn't seem to care who he hurts along the way. By the end of the film I honestly didn't care if he won or lost. If you go in expecting a "Rocky" triumphant finish you're gonna be disappointed.

raw_payload.author: HotTakesAndMilkshakes
raw_payload.content:
What a surprise of a movie this is. It almost feels like it came out of nowhere and took over the world. Is it really that good? Here are the positives and negatives of Marty Supreme:

Positives: The cinematography and storytelling are absolutely fantastic. You easily get invested in the story, which moves with fast-paced, exhilarating energy. Timothée Chalamet deserves all the recognition he's getting. His performance is emotional, tense, and raw, shining in every scene. Odessa A'zion also delivers a powerful performance, perfectly matching the tone of the film.

The writing and dialogue feel believable and well-crafted. Marty Supreme is a divisive film in a fascinating way. You might see it as a story about addiction, a simple tale of a ping pong player, or a depiction of a bad person spiraling into worse situations. I love that original movies like this still exist and get recognition. The story is unique and doesn't end the way you might expect, which is refreshing.

Negatives: Not everything is perfect. Some scenes could have been cut to make it shorter. The anxiety driven momentum of this film can give you a headache. I would have preferred a few slower, more cinematic moments instead of multiple unnecasary sex scenes. That would give the movie some room to breathe.

Marty himself is a complex character , you don't exactly root for him, yet I found myself wanting him to succeed. That's a personal reaction but others might find him unlikable, which could impact their enjoyment.

Overall: This might be my favorite movie of the year. It's far from flawless, but it gets so many things right, and I can see myself rewatching it when I own it for my collection. Marty Supreme is divisive, provocative, and driven by a character you might not love but I still enjoyed every moment. Marty is a charming, fast-talking presence who keeps you glued to the screen.

raw_payload.author: kvggz
raw_payload.content:
A viral Zoom call, an orange blimp and exclusive ping-pong merch.. Hollywood's latest guerilla marketing strategies is what it takes to sell a film in 2026. But what does it take to create one?

Plenty of attention was clearly poured into the 1950s set design yet the needle drop 80s hits feel distracting and out of place. Mr Chalamet's face looks convincingly time-worn (he reportedly even let his vision deteriorate for the role) but just as the production design shows effort while the soundtrack falls short, the performance is undermined by an accent that sounds like a college educated east coast zoomer.

An indulgent runtime, a Tiktok-inspired narrative structure and the sharp absence of anyone worth rooting for all make for a dull watch.

While Mr Chalamet's admirers will surely be satisfied, I felt that even his considerable charisma couldn't pull the film together.

raw_payload.author: jetsetter311-540-775830
raw_payload.content: I'm writing this mainly for my own recollection...I wouldn't call this film entertaining because Marty was, well, a jerk. I wasn't ever rooting for him or hoping things worked out. I did want to see how he got out of every situation and if he managed to make it to his destination. His ultimate aim or goal is never really made clear and his purpose seems to change based on circumstance and then he suddenly changes at the end or we're maybe led to believe that? There's no way Marty doesn't bail on his family the very next day. Or does he stay because he managed to beat Endo so he accomplished what he needed to? I don't know...it feels like the overall story could have been better defined but, regardless, I laughed and enjoyed the hijinks and ok, fine, I was entertained for 2.5 hrs.

raw_payload.author: phx_andrew
raw_payload.content:
I came into this not knowing much other than I'm not a huge Chalemet fan. I loved it. It was totally manic and I couldn't keep my eyes off it. Yes, he is a horrible person and while I wasn't exactly rooting for him I also found I didn't want him to fail. I have to attribute that to the acting. I had 2 literal gasp out loud moments which I always love in a theater. My companions didn't love it as much as I did. They both criticized a lack of plot. raw_payload.author: Stargrazingreviews
raw_payload.content: Josh Saffdie continues his excellent run of movies by maintaining his trademark suffocating atmosphere in which a series of interwoven plotlines build into a never ending anxiety attack. Chalamet is a tour de force as Marty, playing his narcissism perfectly where you end up routing for him despite making bad and self centered decisions at all every turn. The anachronistic 80s synth pop/rock soundtrack is sensational and the film lets you know it's willing to get weird from the outset with two standout scenes (sperm and honey).

raw_payload.author: willboroughs
raw_payload.content: This film made me remember why i loved movies so much in the first place. Watching it at the theatre with an amazing audience gave me a ton of inspiration and energy for the new year. Timothee Chalamet embodied the role of Marty McFly flawlessly and I throuoghly enjoyed the ride with the awesome characters. Thumbs up from me.

raw_payload.author: kjproulx
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is a fantastic piece of filmmaking by director Josh Safdie. He is a filmmaker I've long admired for the projects he made with his brother, especially Uncut Gems and Good Time. This marks the first film I've seen that he has directed on his own, and he absolutely knocked it out of the park. Safdie's confident direction and sharp/quick storytelling abilities are on full display here. Here's why I loved this movie so much and why I believe it stands as one of the very best films of 2025

Marty Supreme is a film about Marty Mauser, played by Timothée Chalamet, who dreams of becoming a table tennis champion. Set in 1952 New York, Marty balances his job as a shoe salesman with competing in ping-pong tournaments. He travels to London for the British Open, where he faces off against various opponents and from there, it becomes a series of one stressful scene after another. The energy this film has makes its two and a half hour runtime breeze by. That is my favourite type of filmmaking, when a film is so good while not ever giving you a chance to catch your breath.

One thing some people may complain about is just how unlikeable Marty is as a character, but for me, that's exactly what makes the film so engaging. Timothée Chalamet gives a performance that completely pulls you in while also making you actively dislike him. I genuinely didn't like Marty as a person, yet I still found myself rooting for him in a strange way. That push and pull adds a lot of tension and keeps the story compelling. All of this is elevated by sharp editing choices and top-notch pacing throughout the film.

There are several twists that genuinely caught me off guard, especially in a film where I wasn't expecting many surprises. The movie is endlessly entertaining thanks to its relentless, kinetic energy. This very well could be the performance that earns Timothée Chalamet his first Oscar, and I believe the film deserves all the Best Picture praise it has been receiving. I haven't experienced a movie quite like this in 2025, and in terms of pacing, it feels like a perfect blend of Uncut Gems and The Social Network. I can't recommend this one enough.

raw_payload.author: hellorandy1
raw_payload.content:
It's the same beat over and over pounded onto the screen. Chalamet is amazing as always, but this character is so unlikable and mean spirited. I didn't find it enjoyable to watch someone walk all over other people for his own gain. The final scenes felt contrived and seemed to ask the audience to forgive too much.

I was exhausted by the end.

raw_payload.author: elmondi
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is a fast-paced, immersive experience that had me on the edge of my seat for every second of its runtime.

The plot is so intense and unpredictable that it barely gives you a moment to breathe. It's wildly engaging and weirdly insane-the whole thing feels like a fever dream. The music and visuals are paired perfectly, elevating the experience at every turn.

Marty himself is a complex character. He's arrogant, self-centered, overconfident, manipulative-a full-blown narcissist who believes in his dream above all else. He's deeply unlikable, yet I found myself strangely rooting for him. I have an unexplainable love-hate relationship with the character. The trail of destruction left in the wake of Marty's pursuit of his dream somehow never manages to derail him. He ruins several lives, valuing people solely based on how useful they are to his ambition. He cares for almost no one, and even the few people he truly cares about are inevitably hurt by him. Yet, he never pauses to reevaluate his choices or show genuine remorse. Marty is the kind of character whose writing you respect, while silently hoping you never encounter someone like him in real life. His destructive tendencies, combined with his relentless determination, make him a force to be reckoned with.

The story is laced with strong themes and messaging. It forces you to confront the cost of ambition and ask yourself whether you're willing to make the sacrifices required to achieve your dreams-and not just sacrifices in your own life, but sacrifices involving the people around you. Marty is ambition taken to its extreme. He severs emotional attachments, ensuring there's nothing to slow him down or hold him back from getting what he wants. You almost have to admire his resolve. I've rarely seen a character ruin the lives of others in such spectacular fashion and seemingly get away with it. But does he really?

The ending delivers the first slow, quiet moment in the entire film. Marty achieves his dream, but not in the way-or on the scale-he imagined. He returns home to face the consequences of his actions. For the first time, he stops running.

I've got to say, this film is insane. Watching Marty interact with others-speaking tactlessly, without an ounce of emotional intelligence-was genuinely mind-blowing. He's brazen, sharp-mouthed, and completely self-absorbed. He truly cares for no one but himself.

And somehow... I love and hate him.

raw_payload.author: filmephile
raw_payload.content:
While the performances are certainly good, and the frenetic and energetic cinematography and spirit of the film are nice, the story itself feels lacking. A young man pursues his dream of becoming a table-tennis champion, and along the way there are challenges. That's pretty much it. Nothing groundbreaking. It's basically a common sports film we've all seen for the umpteenth time made to soothe the masses. Throughout the runtime, I remember thinking that it's both too long and contains things that work. Quite a few times I wondered when the film would conclude.

The film should've been tabled (tennis) for editing. The acting across the board is good and charming, with Chalamet doing all he could here, and Paltrow giving a subtle, winsome turn as a glamorous movie star. But the film does ultimately feel overlong, melodramatic, and quite formulaic. It's just another underdog-aspiring-to-be-a-champion film that just doesn't quite stick its landing. Just because it's based (loosely) on the life of Marty Reisman doesn't automatically make it interesting either. It doesn't feel as inspiring as it aims to be. The action scenes often feel unearned and like they were added in solely for shock value rather than feeling organic. There are scenes of characters yelling and fighting over basically nothing. And the subject matter, table-tennis, is not in itself all that compelling. While yes, the way the sport scenes are filmed is engaging and dynamic, and it's in these scenes where the cinematography shines, ultimately you're kind of just watching a guy hit a ball back and forth across tables. The main character is ornery and isn't really likeable and is rather immature for much of the film, which can be hard to watch. There is a scene involving a paddle and a butt that felt anachronistic and rather out of place.

When the film ended, I remembered thinking, is that it? And, I don't want to spoil things, but when you see the ending you'll understand: it sort of misses the mark. It's all just so predictable. Bigger swings, pun intended, could've been taken here to make the film less generic.

I did enjoy that the film felt sort of deliberate at times. A number of characters are played by actors who themselves are in a way, the characters they play. For instance, you have O'Leary, a real-life businessman, portraying a businessman; and Kawaguchi, a real-life table-tennis player, portraying a table-tennis player. This choice brought the film a kind of verisimilitude that was an interesting choice. Further, it isn't afraid to poke fun at stereotypical American entitlement, stereotypical Jewish avarice and miserliness and aggression, and the gritty reality of how the American Dream isn't necessarily all it's cracked up to be. There are some touching and funny moments sprinkled throughout the film. And the music and cinematography are nice.

Overall, the film is just alright to slightly above average. Charming performances, nice visuals, and some interesting social commentary carry the film.

raw_payload.author: falcon-61763
raw_payload.content:
I can already tell I'm gonna be in the minority with this movie. Safdie thinks he's Tarantino but doesn't have the dialogue, the charm, the wit, the smart camera, or the arrogant-yet-likable characters.

So many different storylines that didn't matter, so many dropped characters that I wish we had spent more time with. And at the end of the day, our main character doesn't grow or change at all.

raw_payload.author: dweston-38669
raw_payload.content:
I saw this at the Curzon,Colchester with both Nigel and Deirde.

It starts off well with a zippy pace and outstanding production design courtesy of Mr Sissy Spacek and best friend of David Lynch, Jack Fisk who also designed many great movies including Days of Heaven and There Will Be Blood.

Timothee Chalamet gives a nervy, sweaty performance, like Adam Sandler did in Uncut Gems, and he gives this loathsome worm of a character a gravitas that is in part part Billy Liar,the aforementioned Adam Sandler and Reymond Berry from Barry Lyndon- the audience may not like or sympathise with these odd ,unlikeable men but they certainly can't stop watching them get into scrapes and misdemeanours that by the end they go on a journey to possible redemption.

Chalamet is ably supported by both strong female support in Gwyneth Paltrow and Odessa A' zion. The latter excellent in a role of both childlike naivety and stupidity.

The ping pong scenes were well staged but I wouldn't say I was on the edge of my seat , especially at the end match because I really didn't care if Marty won at all.

Where the film falters is a draggy midsection where he and Rachel go looking for a dog to get a reward that was utterly redundant and pointless.

In fact the whole film could have shed an hour off its elephantine length much like The Brutalist did a year ago.

Like The Brutalist, this is a critics/awards kinda film where all the attributes- acting, directing,photography, design are all top notch but are all in cahoots looking for a story .....or a script editor.

Like The Brutalist there are things I admire but I really wouldn't watch it again.

I had high expectations for this film. The trailer was amazing, and it's what drew me in and set the bar high. But man, oh man, how they've exceeded my already high expectations. Never has a film ever done so.

This movie isn't what you think it's about. But by the end you should know. Beautifully well done, and I can see why the film is already nominated for awards before even being released. 2025 has been a year of really great films. Let the games begin.

raw_payload.author: MorfenZ
raw_payload.content:
Timothée Chalamet once again proves why he's one of the greatest actors of his generation. The range of emotions he displays in this film is absolutely brilliant-the fire in his eyes feels real, raw, and captivating. His chemistry with his costar gwyneth adding an extra layer of depth to the film.

The direction is strong and thoughtful, with beautifully crafted storytelling, sharp dialogue, and writing that keeps you engaged. The narrative itself is layered, intertwining multiple stories that all converge into one powerful journey about pursuing goals and personal greatness. Beneath its surface, the film is rich with values and meaningful insights for those willing to look deeper.

While the story has some imperfections, Timothée Chalamet's performance carries the entire film. For a 2024 production with a likely modest budget, it delivers impressive drama and comedic moments that feel authentic and memorable. This is a must-see film, and I wouldn't be surprised if it becomes a strong Oscar contender.

raw_payload.author: Kosekerem
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is a pure chaos film starring Timothée Chalamet, with a relentless pace that never lets the audience breathe for even a second. It feels like a movie that perfectly embodies Josh Safdie's signature style-especially in its final act, which plays out at a constant heart rate of 200 BPM. But whether it's for you or not... that depends.

The dialogue feels like two sides firing all their ammunition at once. Still, thanks to sharp writing, subtle humor, and strong performances, the film flows effortlessly without ever losing momentum.

This is not a movie that strictly follows its main plot. Instead, it focuses on the messy, intricate, and chaotic road leading to it. If you care more about a clear, linear story, you might feel disconnected at times. But if you enjoy the journey itself, the film constantly pulls you back in.

At its core, the story follows a professional table tennis player obsessed with his goal and how much he's willing to sacrifice to achieve it. Chalamet's performance captures that hunger and ambition perfectly-so well that you sometimes feel that final sense of satisfaction might truly be worth everything.

The supporting characters add color and texture, but the spotlight never leaves Chalamet. He dominates every scene, and for me, this performance firmly places him among the strongest Oscar contenders of the year.

The music is carefully chosen, the visuals are not flashy but full of vision, and the tournament scenes genuinely make you feel inside the chaos.

The main downside is that the constant misfortune surrounding the main character can feel slightly forced at times. Still, even when the film loses you, it somehow manages to pull you right back in.

Final thoughts: Timothée Chalamet should win the Oscar.

Josh Safdie has fully mastered his craft-this feels even more solid than Uncut Gems.

raw_payload.author: GerardoMenmar
raw_payload.content:
After directing alongside his brother Bennie the acclaimed Good Time and Uncut Gems, Josh Safdie returns to working solo to deliver a masterful film about ambition, arrogance, and the nightmare of the American Dream.

As a faux biopic-loosely inspired by the life of ping-pong icon Marty Reisman-Timothée Chalamet plays Marty Mouser, a talented yet deeply narcissistic table tennis player who believes he deserves everything. However, in his pursuit of success, the world around him strikes back, offering a harsh lesson in humility and a brutal glimpse into the savage capitalist system, where achieving your goals may require entering a profoundly rotten world.

From the opening credits, Safdie maintains a relentless pace, unfolding the events of the character's life through moments of sharp, acidic humor that are as impactful as they are uncomfortable due to their sadistic outcomes. In the tradition of Citizen Kane or The Social Network, where the protagonist rises through ethically questionable decisions, and with a stylistic approach reminiscent of Scorsese's After Hours and The Color of Money, as well as the cinema of Paul Thomas Anderson, Safdie delivers a true masterclass in technical and narrative filmmaking. The film feels like something straight out of the 1970s or 1980s, with its dark tone and unapologetically antiheroic protagonist.

Without a doubt, Chalamet gives the performance that cements him as one of the finest actors of his generation. Despite how grating his character can be, he manages to make us empathize with everything he goes through. It would not be surprising if he were to finally win the Academy Award for Best Actor after several nominations in recent years. The supporting cast is equally noteworthy, with Gwyneth Paltrow delivering an excellent turn in a return to more serious roles following her time in Marvel, businessman Kevin O'Leary proving surprisingly effective in his first film role, and Fran Drescher appearing in a non-comedic part that goes against the type audiences are accustomed to seeing her in.

On a technical level, everything is superbly balanced, highlighted by Darius Khondji's cinematography, with its dark tones, heavy grain, and constantly moving camera. Daniel Lopatin's score is equally impressive; although the film is set in the 1950s, it draws on musical textures from the 1970s and 1980s to reinforce the stylistic atmosphere Safdie aims to evoke.

One of the strongest contenders of the awards season, Marty Supreme is a great film for lovers of New Hollywood cinema and a reaffirmation that Josh Safdie is one of the new heirs to American filmmaking, in this portrait where the pursuit of dreams often reveals itself to be more of a nightmare.

raw_payload.author: rickchatenever
raw_payload.content:
Last year he played Bob Dylan. Now he's playing champion-level ping-pong.

Is there any challenge Timothée Chalamet can't master?

Then again, playing the role of Marty Mauser - otherwise known as "Marty Supreme" - is an act of supreme self-confidence.

Such chutzpah as they might say in the Lower East Side Jewish neighborhood where the fast-talking 23-year-old lives in his mother's apartment.

Loosely based on the life of Marty Reisman, a table-tennis champ and brash hustler in the early 1950s, director/co-writer Josh Safdie creates pitch-perfect period settings around Chalamet's performance that sucks all the air out of whatever room he's in.

The faces of the large cast add to the air of authenticity. With thick New Yawk accents coming out of their mouths, rarely have actors looked less like actors Losing at the Oscars last year, this role - that Chalamet spent seven years perfecting his game for - is a sure thing for an encore nomination.

In an awards season that has left me scratching my head at what dark comedy means, "Marty Supreme" seems a textbook illustration of the genre. And yet, IMDb.com - my guide to all things movie - labels it as an Epic ... Sport ...Period Drama ... Well yes, but ... There are a lot of laughs - some hilarious, some surreal - along the way. Not to mention, nonstop chaos on a pretty epic scale, too.

However you label it, it's a two-hour adrenaline rush as it follows Marty's globe-hopping fever dream of winning the international table tennis grand nationals. His path stretches from ghetto New York neighborhoods to London, Paris and Tokyo. He spends the movie earning, stealing, extorting or pleading for plane fare, entry fees and miscellaneous charges accrued along the way. (He has a fondness for living in luxury.) Marty Mauser is a unique cinematic creation, almost as heroic as he is annoying. Chalamet's portrayal is an instant classic, borderline iconic.

Safdie's fast-moving screenplay, co-written with Ronald Bronstein, surrounds Marty with supporters, victims, lovers, enablers, foils and dupes. (Some characters qualify on all counts.) The story opens with coitus (look it up) between Marty and Rachel Mitzler (Odessa D'Zion) in the backroom of a shoe store. Their on-again, off-again relationship is one for the books. For openers, she's married ... and almost as good as Marty himself when it comes to con games. D'Zion's energized performance conjures memories of Mikey Madison's Oscar-winning star turn in "Anora" last year.

Gwyneth Paltrow tops the co-star credits. She plays fading screen star Kay Stone, whose ice queen demeanor is no match for Marty's relentless pursuit. Kevin O'Leary plays the jerk she's married to, the ultrawealthy owner of a pen and ink company.

With the exception of Fran Drescher as Marty's hypochondriac ma Rebecca, most of the names in the cast aren't well-known. Which isn't to say the performances aren't terrific. They're gritty and street smart, like supporting characters with names like Keitel and Pesci in Martin Scorsese's early films.

It's not just a matter of fine acting, but also director Safdie's confidence and skill behind the camera. (With Benny Safdie's "The Smashing Machine" also in contention this movie awards season, it's been a very good year for the Safdie brothers.) As much as he's a player and a hustler, Marty Mauser is also a narcissist. Singlemindedly chasing his dreams leaves a lot of people having to pay the bills.

Seems like all of us are learning these days how it feels to be victims of narcissism run amok. Luckily, in Marty's case, he turns out to be capable of self-reflection ... and even empathy.

It all leads to a happy ending that no one could have seen coming, and puts "Marty Supreme" near the top of this reviewer's favorite movies of the year.

raw_payload.author: JaredPono
raw_payload.content: Josh Safdie delivers an incredibly fast-paced energized story about a man who in a post-war new york has a dream to be the best in the world. Fans of Good Time will love this project as Chamalet's electric performance as Marty Mauser has him running around the city desperately trying to find his way to his destiny. raw_payload.author: seulowir
raw_payload.content:
This could have been a brilliant 98 minute movie.

Why do people gush over Safdie? His movies are overlong, padded with pointless scenes that add nothing to the story - that often exist just to shock - and lets moments play out far too long.

Here, as in "Uncut Gems," we spend way too much time with an obnoxious narcissist, with nearly an hour of film time devoted to dead end plot lines that reveal nothing about the character.

Ideas are introduced and thrown away.

He appreciates innovation in the sport (orange balls) but doesn't show any interest in learning about the Japanese paddles and styles, just wants to beat it.

We learn Endo is deaf - does this matter? Nope.

Every moment with Paltrow is pointless, however much she troops through her sad scenes - why even give her dialog? Her character has nothing to say.

The film promises a story about an irritating man with a passion for something who is willing to go to any length to be great, then abandons that to be a dumpster dive into wretchedness.

Safdie is determined to mine the early work Scorsese (the TRUE Marty Supreme) - he thinks he is reinventing "Mean Streets" or "Taxi Driver" but he fails to understand the soul of those films. He needs to rewatch "King of Comedy" until he understands why Marty had to make that movie.

raw_payload.author: cfmxvf
raw_payload.content:
Marry Supreme desperately wants to feel important, but instead it feels exhausting.

From the opening act, the film signals its biggest problem: it's far too long for how little it has to say. Scenes linger well past their usefulness, conversations repeat the same emotional beats, and momentum never truly arrives. The result is not a slow burn-it's a slow drift.

The score is a constant mismatch, often swelling where restraint is needed and flattening moments that should carry weight. Rather than guiding emotion, the music distracts from it, pulling the viewer out of scenes instead of drawing them in.

Most glaring is Kevin O'Leary's appearance, which plays less like casting and more like a cameo stunt that never ends. He isn't inhabiting a character; he's performing Kevin O'Leary. His real-world persona overwhelms the narrative, turning what should be immersion into a reminder that you're watching a movie trying too hard to feel relevant.

There are glimpses of a better film buried underneath-interesting ideas, a premise with potential-but they're smothered by indulgent runtime, tonal confusion, and misjudged choices. By the midpoint, the audience isn't leaning in; they're checking the time.

In the end, Marry Supreme confuses seriousness with significance. It wants to feel grand, meaningful, and provocative-but what it mostly delivers is fatigue.

Final verdict: Overlong, tonally mismatched, and weighed down by distractions. A film that insists on its importance without earning the viewer's attention.

raw_payload.author: Julesbro77
raw_payload.content:
Josh Safdie's cinema has often been defined by acceleration, by bodies pushed to the limit of their own nervous systems. Here, however, velocity becomes less a structural necessity than a posture. Timothée Chalamet's Marty is conceived as a figure of permanent agitation, a subject without interiority, whose arrogance and immaturity are not so much explored as incessantly reiterated. The film observes him, but never truly interrogates the space that produces him.

The secondary characters-Gwyneth Paltrow's decadent Hollywood apparition, Odessa A'zion's wounded and instrumentalized femininity, the opaque presence of Koto Endo-enter the frame with a certain conceptual sharpness, only to remain suspended at the level of signs. They are less characters than figures, less bodies than functions within a system of circulation: desire, power, spectacle. Their vividness is immediate, their persistence negligible.

What initially appears as a study of ambition gradually reveals itself as a problem of duration. The film accumulates events without allowing them to sediment. Montage insists, rhythm accelerates, but meaning stalls. The repetition of intensity replaces development; movement substitutes for transformation. The spectator is not invited to think, only to keep up.

Safdie's mise en scène, once grounded in a productive friction between chaos and necessity, here seems increasingly autonomous, almost decorative. The film believes in its own urgency more than in what it shows. Excess is no longer a condition of existence, but a stylistic guarantee-proof, perhaps, of seriousness.

In the end, the film does not collapse; it exhausts itself. Its energy turns tautological, its ambition circular. What remains is a cinema of agitation without rupture, of gesture without consequence-an object that demands attention while offering little resistance to thought. I did find the soundtrack remarkable both for the use of nostalgic pieces and the original soundtrack.

raw_payload.author: jrr-69009
raw_payload.content:
As someone who rarely writes reviews despite seeing so much great movies throughout my life, I do feel like this movie demands one. To put this film into words is almost impossible, for it is genuinely one of the best and most unique film experiences I have ever had. Every single detail, down to the last square inch, is so perfect and masterfully crafted that it would be disrespectful to try and describe it. Without exaggeration, this is one of the best films ever made. It keeps you both constantly entertained and deeply anxious throughout the entire thing, moves at an energetic and relentlessly fast pace nonstop despite it's 2 hours and 30 minute runtime. The movie has a powerful overall story, realistic and emotional character conflicts, deeply meaningful symbolism, and exceptional character development as well, and some moments that are worthy of being iconic to cinema itself. The ambiance of the film is similar to that of Josh Safdie's other films "Good Time" and "Uncut Gems", but even those fantastic films pale in comparison to what is achieved here. And once again, the Vaporwave pioneer and legendary producer Daniel Lopatin composes the soundtrack, with his score perfectly capturing the film's emotional range, shifting between upbeat triumph and suffocating dread, creating a retro soundscape that amplifies every feeling the film tries to convey. The final match sequence is one of the most stressful scenes I have ever experienced in a movie, it genuinely made me feel as if I was Marty, fighting for survival, knowing that everything he endured had led to this one moment, and that he had only one chance to make it all count. Everybody's character contributed something unique to the movie, as every character mattered and advanced the plot in some way to make sure no talent was wasted, with Gwyneth Paltrow's tragic and yet deeply human character adding a layer of complexity to the film and Marty's journey and Odessa A'zion giving a career-best performance that is arguably the second best performance in the entire film next to Timothee's, with her raw and vulnerable performance making her character and her own character's story impossible to ignore and essential to the story's momentum. Even non-actors such as Tyler The Creator and Kevin O'Leary left lasting impressions, with Tyler's natural screen presence creating an effortless chemistry with Timothee, and making their scenes together some of the most fun and engaging throughout the piece, and it would be great if he continued acting as he has potential to become not just the musical legend that he already is, but craft a new legacy in the world of film. And Mr Wonderful himself (Kevin O'Leary) comes through in his role, since his naturally condescending personality makes it easy for him to not even have to act, making his character and what it represents real and special to the film. Mind you, these are just SOME of the standout performances the film has to offer. Timothee's acting is at its peak here and makes the movie shine, and as someone who has been following his work for years I can confidently say this is his best acting to date. In fact, his work is so powerful that the ending scene made me cry, not because of sadness, but because of the impact of his expression that seemed so natural and almost impossible to replicate. Easily film of the year and will be remembered for decades to come, I highly recommend everyone to watch it.

P. S: there are way more things I wish I could have said in this review but I tried to make it spoiler-free.

raw_payload.author: LeonardoOliva69
raw_payload.content:
When you finish watching this movie you immediately have to think that what you saw, without knowing who the directors are, is a continuation of what was observed in "Uncut Gems". That's what happened to me with this movie.

A priori, a story with little to tell about a sport that is not so massive or interesting for the majority. The reality, a marketing campaign never seen before and a single objective: the Oscar for Timothée Chalamet, one with which he has been flirting for a while and it seems that this year will be his year.

Personally, I liked the movie, but I won't deny that it is difficult to watch at times. Difficult because 150 minutes is a long time and difficult because it is difficult to see Marty do everything wrong all the time everywhere. It is super uncomfortable to see what happened with "Uncut Gems", in much of the film it is impossible to empathize with the main character. He's a bad guy, he's a narcissist, he's a scammer, he's a criminal in every sense and by some miracle he doesn't end up in prison or dead.

However, I consider that the movie wants that and achieves it, he is not a hero, nor does he want to be, he is a survivor of the post-World War II world who is looking for his place and his moment. It is a time when no one gives you anything and everyone must get ahead on their own.

Chalamet is excellent, every film he makes is well chosen, he wants to be a remembered actor and he is on the right path, whether he likes it or not, whether he is likeable or not. His talent is undeniable and he is possibly the Leo Di Caprio of his generation.

Gwyneth Paltrow, in her return to cinema after only doing Marvel films, is quite good in her role and makes us feel her vulnerability and difficulties.

Odessa A'zion is the one who gets the biggest applause in Chalamet's shadow with a sober, desperate performance, another survivor, another small-time scammer. There really are no good people in this story, just people looking for their place.

I feel that the rest of the secondary characters are not fully utilized and their plots are lost in the whirlwind of the roller coaster that is the movie. It doesn't stop at any time and that is to the detriment of the rest of the characters such as Marty's mother played by the beloved Fran Drescher, which is only a nominal role and not used.

The editing is great, it feels super real. The table tennis matches (because let's not forget that the movie is about that too) are seen with a realism that is hard to believe and at every point we feel the tension, especially the two confrontations with the Japanese Endo.

The music is another interesting section, it is anachronistic, we are in the 50s and the entire soundtrack is from the 80s, clearly showing that Marty is not a man of his time, he lives ahead of his time. That nervousness, that anxiety, is very well represented throughout the film.

The ending, unlike the Safdie's previous works, is a positive, bright ending, with a Marty who understands his place and who evolves to be a better person. Maybe this part falls short for me due to the fact that after so many bad things he did, the only punishment was a couple of blows and not being able to play in the table tennis world championship.

In short, a film that is headed for awards this year and that will surely give Chalamet his first Oscar for Best Actor.

raw_payload.author: Caveboi
raw_payload.content: Personally, this type of genre is something I'd easily pick out but on a random Thursday, why not? And I went in to watch without any expectations-and I really enjoyed it. I love how I couldn't predict anything that would happen next, and that's what I miss most whenever I watch films especially from this generation.

raw_payload.author: ferguson-6
raw_payload.content:
Greetings again from the darkness. We give thanks to the movie Gods for filmmakers like Josh Safdie, as there is zero chance that he will ever make a movie that bores us. This is his first solo movie in many years after frequent collaborations with his brother, Benny (UNCUT GEMS, 2019; GOOD TIME, 2017). But fear not, because working alongside the writer-director are other frequent collaborators: screenwriter Ronal Bronstein, cinematographer Darius Khondji, composer Daniel Lopatin, and costume designer Miyako Bellizzi.

The film opens in 1952 New York City where Marty Mauser (two-time Oscar nominee Timothee Chalamet) is working as a shoe salesman, barely managing to stay a half-step ahead of bill collectors. Marty uses the shoe storage room for quickies with childhood friend Rachel (Odessa A'zion, SITTING IN BARS WITH CAKE, 2023), who is married to a stereotypical inner-city brute named Ira (Emory Cohen). Rachel much prefers Marty, while also realizing his big dreams of greatness and grandeur prevent him from ever settling down for family life. It's startling how quickly we come to understand the personalities here.

Marty manages to get himself to the table tennis championship tournament where his skill in the game is almost as obvious as his too-big-for-his-britches persona. He's all American in that his arrogance shines through his fast-talking entitlement ... diametrically opposite to his Japanese opponent in the finals. Marty's hustler instincts score him a luxury hotel upgrade as well as an introduction to once-popular movie star Kay Stone (Oscar winner Gwyneth Paltrow) and her husband (played by "Shark Tank" investor Kevin O'Leary). Marty smooth-talks one of them into bed, while attempting to fast-talk the other into a business deal.

If you've seen previous Safdie films, you know he thrives on a frenetic pace and characters who create chaos. As an example, we are subjected to a cringe-inducing honey-licking visual, as well as a bathtub accident and related dog incident, both succeeding in generating shock value. This is a hectic story about a chaotic guy (supposedly loosely based on real life ping pong player/entertainer Marty Reisman). After losing in the tournament finals, our Marty endures a crazy year that includes ping pong against a seal and performing halftime showboating at Globetrotters games - his work properly described as a vaudeville act.

Safdie sprinkles in some familiar faces throughout, including NBA legend Geroge Gervin, Fran Drescher (as Marty's mom), Larry Sloman, Sandra Bernhard, and film director Abel Ferrera (in a key role as a dog owner). We even get (musician) Tyler the Creator as Marty's hustling cohort. Sharp-eyed basketball fans might spot Kemba Walker and Tracy McGrady as Globetrotters. Marty's crazy year does culminate with him finding a way to Japan for the next world championship tournament, where kissing a pig becomes the big risk. Some of Marty's antics may sometimes be in the mold of the Three Stooges, but he's clearly very talented at table tennis ... it's just the 'life with integrity' thing that he finds difficult - even drawing in Rachel and Kay. The ending is not unlike many sports movies, but the path Marty and Safdie lead us down is absolutely unique and entertaining.

Opens in theaters on Christmas Day.

raw_payload.author: agwasiak
raw_payload.content:
I went into Marty Supreme with very low expectations, mostly just wanting a cinema trip, and ended up having an amazing time. The premise sounds simple - Marty is a fiercely driven table tennis player chasing greatness - but the film is fast, chaotic, funny, tense, and at times completely unhinged, almost like several genres mashed into one. I've since read that this explosive, unpredictable pace is very much Josh Safdie's style, and it honestly made me keen to watch his other films. Even at nearly two and a half hours, it never felt long. This is not a traditional sports movie like King Richard, or a story about raw talent rising into greatness like Whiplash - it's much messier and more wild, and the film truly commits to that energy. It captures obsession and tunnel vision incredibly well, with Marty in constant go-go-go mode, barely sleeping, always hustling, and as a viewer you really feel that pace and pressure. Following all this madness and fast pacing, the ending (without giving away any spoilers) felt like it finally gave both the viewer and Marty time to breathe, and I found it very satisfying.

I've read in several reviews that people see Marty as selfish or blindly self-focused, and while I get that, I also found it strangely refreshing to watch a story about ambition taken to such an extreme without pretending it's healthy or noble. Timothée Chalamet really impressed me here - I loved his earlier films like Call Me By Your Name, but I haven't really been following his more recent work (and Dune isn't really my thing), and this performance genuinely reminded me how good he is (I honestly wouldn't be surprised if he ends up with the Oscar for this one). You can really feel that this is an A24 film, and I loved the New York setting - it honestly felt like a love letter to the city, not the glossy version but the gritty, hustling, lived-in one, which again I'm hearing is very much in Josh Safdie's style. And the music was the cherry on top - lots of 80s pop that technically doesn't match the period inspiration, but works perfectly for the frantic pace of the film. Honestly, a great recommendation and a brilliant first movie to watch this year.

raw_payload.author: ethanbresnett
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is an absolute triumph, and is potentially my favourite movie to come from a Safdie brother, with the only contender being Uncut Gems.

With Benny Safdie's 'The Smashing Machine' being a more conventional sports biopic released in close proximity to this film, I expected something similar from 'Marty Supreme'. Instead, this film is much more akin to previous Safdie fare like 'Good Time' and 'Uncut Gems'. It is chaotic, stress inducing, propulsive, gripping, and thrilling. It is not a sports biopic at all but more of a supercharged drama following a grifting dreamer as he attempts to reach the top of the table tennis world.

It is a story that will keep you guessing and have you on the edge of your seat, as Josh Safdie blends drama, comedy, emotion, sports, love, family, and much more so effortlessly and expertly. It is sharply written, beautifully shot, well paced, and has a gritty energy to it all.

Timothee Chalamet as Marty is inspired, and he is my pick to win the Academy Award. The character is written so well and has so much depth to him, so it is a good role anyway, but Chalamet elevates it to new heights with his perfect performance. He's just so incredibly electric and unpredictable and knocks it out of the park. The supporting cast are all terrific too, but Chalamet steals every scene he is in, which is virtually every scene in the movie.

Watching this film on the final day of 2025 means I can say without any doubt that it is my film of the year and deserves the many wonderful plaudits it is receiving.

raw_payload.author: FilmdePool
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme revolves entirely around its protagonist, Marty, played by Timothée Chalamet, who will stop at nothing to achieve his goal: making enough money to fly to Japan to compete in the World Table Tennis Championships. His reckless pursuit leads him to constant trouble, directly triggering one drama after another. The film bombards the audience with slightly exaggerated situations in rapid succession, leaving no room to breathe.

In every storyline and every confrontation, big or small, whether with enemies or allies, Marty resorts to all kinds of unethical tactics. Lying, cheating, stealing, scamming, robbing, stopping just short of murder, though that does not mean there is no bloodshed. Knives and guns, laughter and tears. Each incident pushes the emotional intensity higher and higher, mirroring almost exactly the director's earlier work back when he was still part of the Safdie brothers duo, Uncut Gems.

However, after this relentless string of morally bankrupt actions, some might argue that this represents a form of American spirit, though I have my reservations, the protagonist remains completely unchanged. There is no growth, no self-reflection. If anything, he does not even become worse, because this is who he has been from the very beginning.

The director then wraps up the story with another plot device, which I see as emotional manipulation, making the film appear moving in its final moments. In reality, it is little more than sleight of hand. Aside from being visually appealing, Marty as a character offers nothing that truly invites sympathy, which ultimately feels like a missed opportunity.

raw_payload.author: jared-25331
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme (2025) is a sports comedy drama movie co-written and directed by Josh Safdie of the Safdie Brothers and it is loosely inspired by the life and career of American table tennis player Marty Reisman. This is a movie I heard about a couple of months ago and it inspired me to binge through the filmography of the Safdie Brothers and it was a fun ride for me. I just finished watching this movie an hour ago and this is the best movie from the Safdie Brothers or in this case, Josh Safdie.

Positives for Marty Supreme (2025): For a movie based on a guy who played ping pong, Josh Safdie did a phenomenal job with crafting one of the best stories of 2025. It helps that Timothee Chalamet gives one of if not his best performances as Marty Mauser, who is a cocky jackass when you meet him and cares about nothing but be the best at ping pong and somehow Chalamet makes you root for this guy and you want to see him become a good person, while also trying to achieve his goal in life. I also have to say that Gwyneth Paltrow was great as Kay Stone, who is a jackass except much worse than Marty, but she is also very captivating as a character and you want to see her change and see the errors in her ways and her relationship with Marty. However, the biggest surprise in this and every movie in 2025 was Odessa A'zion as Rachel Mizler. This is a woman who I was introduced in a show called Fam (2019) and has shown up in other movies, but this was a star making performance for her and one that is deserving of Best Supporting Actress at the Oscars. She is the thing in Marty's life that gets him to change as he genuinely cares for her and wants to be a better person and their dynamic was the glue that held this movie together. You get other great supporting roles with Kevin O'Leary as Milton Rockwell, Tyler Okonma as Wally, Abel Ferrara as Ezra Mishkin and Fran Drescher as Rebecca Mauser, who help contribute something to the story that makes it feel more engaging for the audience. The ping pong games are surprisingly awesome and this is a sport that I don't really care about, but man Josh Safdie manage to make me care about this sport. And finally, the ending is so heartwarming and a satisfying conclusion to the journey for Marty Mauser as he overcomes his cocky attitude and becomes a better man in life.

Overall, Marty Supreme (2025) is a top tier movie from Josh Safdie as well as one of the best movies of 2025 and I hope to see some nominations at the Oscars for this movie especially for Chalamet and A'zion because they have earned them.

raw_payload.author: CactusJack2222
raw_payload.content: Saw Marty Supreme on New Years Eve and didn't understand the hype after watching. It wasn't bad but also wasn't as great as everybody making it out to be. The pacing felt slow in my honest opinion and the ending credits with Tears For Fears playing felt tacky considering that this movie is supposed to be based in the 50s yet has a song from the 80s being played at the end? Have no idea how anyone could compare this to Josh Safdie's masterpiece Uncut Gems because it was nothing like it, Uncut Gems made you feel something that kept you on the edge of your seat and left you with anxiety lol Marty Supreme left me with nothing and I felt nothing while watching it. Not a bad movie, just not as great as people are saying it is, I'll give it a solid 7/10.

raw_payload.author: griffvest
raw_payload.content: It's a huge, jumbled, overcomplicated mess of people talking over each other for 2 1/2 hours straight. Timothee Chalamet is great (amongst others) but he basically just plays himself on meth. It's Uncut Gems x1000 in terms of anxiety and it just wasn't enjoyable. The protagonist is hardly a protagonist and the tension most of the time leads to nowhere except more tension until the movie just ends. There is literally not a redeeming quality there. There is no point of breath or any kind of payoff until the end and that's barely anything. (Spoiler) He starts crying after looking at his child that he denied was his the whole film. It was so unearned. I would have walked out if I wasn't seeing this film with other people. All the talk about this winning an Oscar is ridiculous and if it does, then the Academy will have lost what little credibility it had left in my opinion.

raw_payload.author: mitzikc
raw_payload.content:
The definition of what cinema should be.

Shock-factor throughout without you even realising you was expecting predictability, because of comparison to every other movie!

No silence throughout. Always music!

Phenomenal acting from all, especially Mr Chalamet.

This makes you realise how much you've missed a fantastic film.

raw_payload.author: Adi_Dobrea
raw_payload.content: Timothée Chalamet's first Oscar for Best Actor? Most likely. Josh Safdie is the Martin Scorsese of our time? Well, hell yeah. One of the best opening sequences? Best picture winner? Well, i wouldn't be surprised at all. Absolutely. An utterly, completely superb film. I've seen a review that said it was like a two and a half hours panic attack. Yeah, in the best sense of the metaphor.

raw_payload.author: Aaron-8422
raw_payload.content: This movie is SO worth the 2 hours and 30 minutes of watching. The way.that you can understand all the plot points, is great. I LOVE how Marty just keeps coming back to things that he left, and Timothee Chalamet did some of the best acting i have seen in a while by a long shot. One thing I wish we had more of is Tyler the creator.

raw_payload.author: Koppa_Young
raw_payload.content:
Ever since I got this app I've been a lot more critical every time I went to the theater to watch cinema. This movie kept me invested from the very start. The camera work is otherworldly, the story is surreal and overall it's up there with the entire MCU and the Cobra Kai finale in terms of pure quality.

Fantastic, lemme watch it 14 more times.

raw_payload.author: Stay_away_from_the_Metropol
raw_payload.content:
What they say is true. Overall, in a universal sense, this is one of the best movies of the year. This is a movie that feels like what movies felt like when the whole world was taking them a lot more seriously. You know, like, in the 70's. Acting, writing, directing, editing, the music...they're all functioning together in a symbiotic regard, and all in a manner which is both loud AF, and disciplined at the same time. The movie has just as much potential to frustrate as it does to fill one with joy, but the frustration does stem from filmmaking error, it stems from intention - a despicable lead character who you still want to partially root for because he is so human. You want to see his journey out, but you may be equally satisfied if he "wins" or "loses", but either way you're going to feel something. This is a sign of a fantastic film.

I am a massive Safdie Brothers fan. When I heard Josh and Benny were going off and doing their own things separate from one another, I at first got the impression that Benny was gonna be the one with the dark side and the knack for continuing to make heavy, provocative stuff with guts. Well, it appears I was wrong. I very much admire the bizarre "sell-out" path that Benny is currently going down, and I can't wait to see what he does next with his scheduled psychedelic family film. But, Josh Safdie has proven with Marty Supreme that he may have been the one with the "mustard" all along, and I mean that spicy brown, with some horseradish in it. Marty Supreme really does play a lot like Uncut Gems, structurally and tonally, but the primary difference is that Marty Supreme is funnier by quite a stretch, and still a stressful ride but a lot less so than Uncut Gems (the most stressful movie I think I have ever seen). I think that by sticking to a similar formula but by toning it back just enough, Josh Safdie may have dialed in the perfect formula for making immaculate cinema with a fully universal appeal for the new generation, floating in a realm with some of the most acclaimed directors of all time - I'm thinking Scorsese in this moment.

The cast is equal parts interesting, refreshing, and immaculately selected. I've never been a Gwyneth Paltrow fan, but she is absolutely perfect for this role. Miss hoity toity. Newest "it girl" Odessa A'Zion puts in phenomenal work and brings a ton of dimension to her relatively limited screen time. And, it's true that this has to be Chalamet's finest performance. I was otherwise previously partial to his collaborations with Luca Guadagnino (Call Me Be Your Name, and my personal fave, Bones and All), but this one really takes the cake. He pulls off the cocky/childish combo in a way I have not seen done this well in a very long time, if ever.

The music score is very important. Daniel Lopatin is one of the great geniuses of electronic music of our time, and personally I am a way bigger fan of his film scores than his proper albums as Oneohtrix Point Never. This one feels quite a bit more like the Uncut Gems score than the Good Time one, as it still feels like it's very floating in a heavily Vangelis-inspired realm, but it's a lot more dynamic, and a lot more listenable. It's really gorgeous and powerful music, and it gives the movie serious, serious character in so many of its scenes, adding to the overall memorability of it all. Big ups to my friend Josh Eustis who apparently mixed, produced, and helped to conduct the orchestra for it! Wow!

Last but not least, this movie has one of those endings that can be perceived in a lot of different ways. My girlfriend and I perceived it entirely differently, and that made me love it even more. I'll just say this, she saw it as a humbling lesson that felt more leaning towards the light, while I saw it as darkness reigning supreme, or metaphorically "getting away with murder". Any ending that can push two intelligent minds in that dramatically different of directions is an ending (and a film) that deserves high credentials.

Marty Supreme is as good as they say. The Safdie Brothers already were the new truth, but Josh Safdie...welcome to the universal A-List. You're a new king. Can't wait to see what the man does with all that power. I trust him more than 95% of filmmakers. I feel like he can only make good stuff. Praise the Safdies.

raw_payload.author: lesleykrauland
raw_payload.content: I'm disappointed with this movie. The characters were self-serving sleezebags and users. The vulgar language and imagery does not add value to the film and was only sensational and a distraction. The plot was incredible- hard to believe that Marty could be so narcissistic to have very little regard for his mother and his pregnant girlfriend. The violence was superfluous. Marty's tears at the end were unbelievable. Not worth seeing a second time.

raw_payload.author: ColinTheGorilla
raw_payload.content: I've truly have been anticipating this film all year and wow this film absolutely delivered. This is easily one of the best films I've see all year and I truly believe this is a masterpiece of a film. I really love the insane direction that Josh Safdie ends up taking this film and the story is a little messy due to all the moving parts but there's so many amazing things it makes it up for me personally. Timothee Chalamet absolutely delivers his best performance of all time in my opinion and it's not even close he has a much growth in this film with his character and it's interesting to see this character constantly get himself into this situations. The rest of the cast is really great as well a there's so many great performances in this film by a lot of the cast. I absolutely found myself to be in awe of this film due to how many emotional swings you'll go through with anxiety and so many other things you'll feel when watching this movie. This film has so much incredible writing and there's so many insane moments which will leave you shocked with what's happening in this film and I also absolutely think the cinematography is top notch here and some of the best of the year and this film looks absolutely beautiful to say the least. The movie is such fast paced which it might be hard for some people to enjoy but I loved this direction due to Marty constantly running away from problems and situations. This movie will leave you on the edge of your seat and that's on exaggeration as well. The production design in this film is absolutely stunning as well and the movie has so many amazing things going for it. The soundtrack is just amazing as well and the movie blares with incredible music throughout the film which makes scenes even more impactful for me. I truly believe this is one of the best film to come out of this year and this is definitely worthy of all the award talk already.

raw_payload.author: gaever-60669
raw_payload.content: Timothy Chalomet is excellent as Marty, a 23-year-old Jewish man brought up in the 1950s Jewish ghetto who is a grifter, a hustler, a salesman and happens to be one of the worlds best ping-pong players. The movie moves relentlessly from one situation to another - sometimes it works and sometimes it doesn't - as Marty reaches for his dream, meeting along the way different character types that we've seen in many movies. It reminded me of Anora - the frenetic pace that amounted to little though like the Anora actress, Chalomet was game and gave his all. If you liked Anora you'd probably like this.

raw_payload.author: logancodel
raw_payload.content:
I love Uncut Gems, so when I heard this movie described as similar, I was looking forward to it. Something was off the whole movie. Timothee Chalamet was not fun to watch the same way Adam Sandler was. When he hurt his friends and family it just seemed sad, not absurd and darkly comedic. I know I shouldn't compare the two movies so closely, but when they're both about overconfident broke a**holes who spend the whole movie telling people they have money they don't have, it's inevitable to compare them. Marty Supreme didn't work for me because the movie wants you to like Marty on some level, even going so far as to attempt a redemption arc, but it's simply too little too late.

Also the fact Chalamet produced the movie gives it a metanarrative of Chalamet being exactly the kind of egotist this movie maligns. He made this movie for himself so he could win an Oscar; that's what I read from it.

raw_payload.author: ttbtrx
raw_payload.content:
Other than showing the life of a narcissist, what's the point of this movie??

Many characters are introduced, built up a bit and then become nothing/irrelevant.

You wonder if the movie is supposed to be taken as a drama, a comedy, an action or a thriller. It's a joke what it is ....

My advice: don't waste your money. Save it for a home movie when you can turn it off at your leisure.

raw_payload.author: z-mgebrishvili
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is the kind of movie that reminds you why going to the theater still matters. It's fearless, funny, and relentlessly alive, blending raw ambition with razor-sharp filmmaking. From the opening moments, the film pulls you into Marty's world with total confidence, never dumbing things down and never losing its pulse.

What really elevates Marty Supreme is its commitment to character. Marty isn't a hollow archetype or a gimmick - he's messy, driven, and magnetic, the kind of protagonist you can't look away from even when you disagree with him. The performances across the board feel fully locked in, balancing intensity with humor in a way that feels effortless but clearly isn't.

Stylistically, the movie is electric. The pacing snaps, the editing has personality, and the direction oozes swagger without ever tipping into self-indulgence. It's bold but precise, chaotic but controlled - a difficult balance that Marty Supreme absolutely nails.

Most of all, the film has something to say about ambition, ego, and the cost of wanting more, and it trusts the audience to keep up. Smart, entertaining, and endlessly rewatchable, Marty Supreme is an instant classic and an easy 10/10.

raw_payload.author: Bk111
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is a blast from start to finish and an easy 9/10 for me. The acting absolutely carries this movie-in the best way. Performances are confident, sharp, and fully committed, bringing real energy and personality to every scene and making the characters genuinely fun to watch.

The screenplay is what really makes it sing. It's super entertaining, well-paced, and packed with moments that keep you locked in. The dialogue is snappy, the humor lands, and the story has a clear sense of direction, building momentum instead of stalling out.

What I appreciated most is that the movie knows exactly what it is and leans into it-bold, character-driven, and unapologetically fun. It's the kind of film that reminds you how powerful great acting and a strong script can be when they're working in sync.

raw_payload.author: Sabaher
raw_payload.content:
This is easily one of the most chaotic and exhilarating movies of the year. We got a film from each of the Safdie brothers this year, and if you had asked me beforehand, I would have bet good money that The Smashing Machine would be my clear favorite over Marty Supreme. An MMA story starring The Rock just seemed far more in my wheelhouse than a movie about ping-pong with Timothée Chalamet.

I could not have been more wrong.

Marty Supreme absolutely captures the frantic, nerve-shredding energy of Uncut Gems. It centers on a lead character who is in a constant state of manipulation-always trying to work everyone around him to get ahead-only now it's in a wildly different and unexpected context. The result is pure, exhilarating chaos.

The protagonist is, by most measures, an absolute menace. He's self-absorbed, impulsive, and completely incapable of thinking through the consequences of his actions. He never knows when to stop talking, never knows when to pull back, and is always scrambling for the next advantage. And yet, he's endlessly fascinating. He's compelling, entertaining, and strangely admirable in flashes, even as he repeatedly sabotages himself. You're watching a captivating train wreck of a person-one who also possesses genuine passion, confidence, and moments of surprising humanity.

What makes the movie so refreshing is that it constantly goes places you don't expect. Calling it a movie about ping-pong or about a guy chasing his dreams doesn't even begin to describe what it's really doing. The story spirals into increasingly outrageous and stressful territory, and you're left repeatedly thinking, "I can't believe this is where this movie is going." That unpredictability is its greatest strength.

This is one of those films where it's almost impossible to properly explain the experience of watching it-you really just have to surrender to it. For me, Marty Supreme completely cut through the noise and stood out as something bold, exhausting, and unforgettable.

raw_payload.author: elicopperman
raw_payload.content:
While I'm admittedly only familiar with the Safdie brother's two most well known features, Good Time and Uncut Gems, even I know they've left somewhat of a big impact on the indie film scene over the last decade or so. So in looking at Josh Safdie's solo directorial debut, Marty Supreme is quite the exhilarating experience that Safdie fans would come to expect from their line of work.

The general storyline of a young man who navigates life as a ping pong champion in 1950s New York City details the titular character's dreams of ambition, his rocky relationships, and the pursuit of success. Much of Safdie's trademark stress inducing intensity and drowning character interactions remain highly intact, so much so that the detestable Marty Mauser is just as compelling to watch as he is detestable. Similar to how Uncut Gems explored the hustle and bustle of American counter culture, the movie entertains through its countless plot threads woven into Marty's overly chaotic life, from his affair with childhood friend Rachel Mizler, to getting sexually tangled into former actress Kay Stone, to finding any ends meet for a $1,500 fine during a world ping pong championship tournament. Because of all the lingering consequences that come from these various circumstances caused by Marty's never ending hustle, the plot leaves no stone unturned and always has the next worst possible outcome waiting just around the corner.

Much of the success in how much of a convincing sleazebag Marty is comes from Timothée Chalamet's impeccable performance. In capturing the charismatic essence of someone constantly conning people into trusting him despite their reasonable doubts, Chalamet sells the intensity right on screen alongside sharing strong screen time with his costars. Gwyneth Paltrow and Odessa A'zion play Kay and Rachel well in contrast to differentiating love lives for Marty, one with more of a facade and the other with impending misfortune, and Kevin O'Leary is delightfully scummy as Kay's businessman husband Milton Rockwell, further adding into Marty's desire for impressing those who are just as susceptible to abusing him. Many of the remaining cast are a delight for how much their characters embody distinct yet equally sleazy human practices, arguably making Marty's whole life more of a sport than table tennis ever was. Add on the film's naturalistic recreation of early 50s New York and what we're witnessing on screen is nothing short of fascinating human ambition.

Marty Supreme is not only a must watch for die hard Safdie fanatics, but it's an endlessly captivating character study that will leave you as intrigued as it will also leave you stunned. It's the type of hyper intense adrenaline inducing feature that is nonetheless accessible for witnessing how one ambitious sleazeball can make an outcome for all the right and wrong reasons. Here's to Chamalet and Safdie getting at least some prestigious award recognition for this incredible effort, and what a marvelous way to cap off 2025.

raw_payload.author: bastille-852-731547
raw_payload.content:
I've become a big fan of Timothee Chalamet as an actor, since he's been in a number of great films, from both "Dune" films to "A Complete Unknown" to "Call Me By Your Name," "Lady Bird," "Little Women" and more. As a result, I was certainly eager to see this gritty sports drama from Josh Safdie in which Chalamet plays a young ping-pong player trying to hustle his way to a championship tournament in Japan. Chalamet's performance is very strong, as he plays a uniquely unlikeable character with swagger and confidence. Unfortunately, the rest of the story doesn't always work as well as his performance does, even compared to an even more "chaotic" Safdie film like "Uncut Gems."

The reason why the screenplay has some structural issues is that while the ping-pong scenes are entertaining and there are a handful of scattered other entertaining moments here and there, much of the story isn't interesting enough to drag on for 2.5 hours, especially given that Marty isn't always a particularly interesting character. The supporting characters are also not particularly engaging and sometimes seem underwritten. Safdie's style at creating chaos generally works, but unlike something like "Uncut Gems," that high-tension style feels a bit more detached--rather than directly integrated to--some of the narrative. I still think Chalamet did a good job with the material he was given, but the plot was comparatively a bit weaker. 6/10.

He even cheats on the girlfriend who's cheating on her husband with him, and I'm sorry but him getting mired in one asinine fiasco after another isn't interesting. And as-if him bawling his eyes out over his bastard baby in the end was the character arc we were waiting for, please.

raw_payload.author: romidavid-07113
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme tries to be a fast-paced character study, but ends up confusing chaos with depth. The film does not develop its characters in a meaningful way and instead relies on stereotypes and very shallow goals. Because of this, the actors are given little to work with, and many performances feel flat.

The story itself is messy and unfocused. Scenes feel random and rarely connect to each other in a way that moves the story forward. Characters appear and disappear without explanation, and there are no smooth transitions or clear consequences. Rather than feeling bold or experimental, the structure feels careless.

Timothée Chalamet's performance didn't really work for me. The accent is inconsistent, and there is little emotional depth or inner conflict in the character. He never feels fully believable or grounded, and the same problem affects much of the supporting cast.

The film also leans heavily on stereotypes without questioning or challenging them, which makes certain portrayals feel uncomfortable rather than thoughtful. Without strong characters or a clear story, Marty Supreme ends up feeling loud and busy, but empty at its core.

raw_payload.author: redban02
raw_payload.content:
I went to see Marty Supreme on 12/29/2025. Overall, I like it.

I'll start with the good -- the movie has a well-done 1950s setting; all the costumes and period props look right. I really like the ping-pong scenes that occur in London and Japan. Those parts reminded me of the presentation of MMA fights in "The Smashing Machine" (and that's not an insult. Those scenes are one of the things that Smashing Machine got right). I knew nothing about ping-pong/table tennis before this movie, and I've never been interested in it. But I found the movie makes the sport look credible and respectable ... The movie also uses some 1980s music at various points, and the effect isn't jarring. The music actually works

The plot takes a little while to get into, and even past the midway point, I found myself getting a little skeptical about where the movie was going. I was also a little put-off by the lead character's unlikeable traits for most the movie (not to mention the punchable face) ... But in the end, the movie has a excellent payoff. The ending is thrilling and satisfying. The lead character becomes someone easy to root for. The message of the movie hits the mark

And maybe most of all - I was impressed by Chalamet. His acting is smooth and impressive. His facial reactions and vocal delivery couldn't have been better, whether it's during his anger outbursts or his determined expressions during the ping-pong games. He really makes a lot of scenes seem so real.

In spite of all my ravings, though, I can't give a perfect score -- though Chalamet's acting is good, there were some scenes where his physical size became a bit awkward. He's obviously small and thin, yet the movie tries to make him appear macho at times . I didn't find him to be a credible companion to Gwenyth Paltrow (note: I actually think this role would have been great for a young Leo DiCaprio in the Catch Me If You Can era)

plot-wise, the movie gets darker and scarier than it should have. Without spoiling, I'll just say that in between the ping-pong stuff, the movie basically becomes a straight crime-thriller, with stuff involving a lost dog. The intensity of those scenes creates a jarring, out-of-place feeling

Lastly - I didn't like the pregnant girl. She gets a lot of screen-time, which was necessary for the ending. But her character could've been a lot less annoying

8.5/10.

raw_payload.author: EUyeshima
raw_payload.content: Director Josh Safdie doesn't like to stay within the confines of one genre or for that matter, within the historical expectations in one period of time. Generally, such revisionist sensibilities can spell disaster for a personality-driven film like this, but Safdie (along with co-writer Ronald Bronstein) revels in the anachronistic spirit of this 2025 period dramedy and creates a unique anti-underdog story that rides on the pervasive cynicism of the title character. Set in 1952 and based on a true story, the plot centers on brazenly selfish Marty Mauser, a young hustler initially stuck selling shoes but with big dreams of becoming the world table tennis champion. Taking no prisoners, he makes it to the London championships but in the finals, loses to a zen Japanese player. Marty seeks revenge and becomes desperate for a rematch in Tokyo. What happens in between the tournaments is challenging to summarize because the loop-de-loop, often criminal escapades take on their own frenetic energy with a gallery of characters which Safdie has cast with an unusual array of unexpected personalities. Even more than his impressive turn as Dylan in "A Complete Unknown", Timothée Chalamet is fully committed to showing Marty to be an unscrupulous Machiavellian and despite his frequent political incorrectness, managing to develop a rooting interest in his character. In a surprisingly welcome return, Gwyneth Paltrow is stylishly effective as an aging film star unhappily married to a rich industrialist and in the midst of a hopeful stage comeback. Entering a torrid affair with Marty, she plays the smallish role of Kay like a mid-century Joan Crawford. As not quite the girl he leaves behind, Odessa D'Azion brings heart and grit to a role that willingly breaks down the victim stereotype. I wish Fran Drescher had more screen time as Marty's put-upon mother because her exhausted knowingness was palpable. Other recognizable faces include Kevin O'Leary of "Shark Tank" ideally cast as Kay's manipulative husband; director Abel Ferrara as a dog-owning gangster; Tyler, the Creator as Marty's only true friend; author Pico Iyer as a tournament official; and Sandra Bernhard as a nosy neighbor. Production designer Jack Fisk and cinematographer Darius Khondji do stellar work capturing the rich look and feel of the early 1950's in multiple locations. The 1980's-heavy soundtrack from Daniel Lopatin works really well almost inexplicably. My one complaint is that the 150-minute running time felt rather long in the middle section.

raw_payload.author: apexadam

It is unlikely an accident that this rare, iconic, and dark foray into human depravity was released during Christmas week. I'm still decompressing and washing my soul from viewing it hours later.

No doubt Marty Supreme is a masterpiece of writing, production, and acting. It digs an endless array of dysfunction, brokenness, and psychological depravity from the depths of the human self-centered soul. This is not a feel-good movie about Ping Pong.

It lands as one of the best movies I hated at the same time. That's what makes it so visceral.

Historical pieces of Table Tennis from its post-war past are scattered throughout the movie without being explicitly stated.

Genius movie and not for those who want to feel good afterward.

raw_payload.author: pamelajackson-60384
