raw_payload.author: Doctor-of-Nothing
raw_payload.content:
Like Marty, the film that bears his name has no heart. It's all plot, no story. A runaway Rube Goldberg machine that keeps the character conning and the camera charging through relationships, conventions, and common sense, only to leave the audience behind to sift through the debris for the film's emotional point or purpose.

Safdie's talent and style is evident throughout. He's a good, highly kinetic director. And Chalamet has probably never been better. He's an actor who shows no fear and here he embraces Marty's shamelessness and cruelty with glee.

Unfortunately, it's all in service to an overstuffed and overlong shaggy dog story (with an actual shaggy dog) that rinses and repeats Marty's callousness in incident after incident that batters, and frankly, bores. The character never stops moving or talking, but ends up standing still. His ping-pong shots have an arc, but he doesn't. And the completely unearned finale, despite what the writers might believe, doesn't lend him one.

This is Catch Me If You Can without the joie de vivre. The Loneliness of the Long Distance Runner without the rebellion. It glitters and shines and catches the eye, but it's not another uncut gem. It's costume jewelry.

raw_payload.author: watchmovies247365

raw_payload.author: Seps786
raw_payload.content:
Marty supreme is a fun movie where the main character makes horrible decisions throughout the whole movie and every side character also makes horrible decisions when he is involved.

Fantastic performances, set design and drama really makes the world of the 1950's feel alive. With a believable story about Japan rising from the dark times of the post war with a sports star.

Marty himself has a clear conscious but has the sports mentality of doing anything to achieve his goals. His ego and poor decision making creates the drama in the first place so begging for forgiveness really does hit hard.

The run time of the movie could have been a bit longer however, the actual ping pong aspect of the movie does get lost, intentionally or not. Marty is shown to love the sport but we do not get to experience why it means so much to him and why he is fighting so hard to get it back.

Overall a solid movie, some memorable moments but lacks depth or complexity.

raw_payload.author: esginyaman
raw_payload.content:
I won't comment on the direction, actors or film as they were all fantastic. I definitely recommend it.

The film features an annoying yet charismatic protagonist, which makes it unique. Mauser is portrayed as a jerk and rather apathetic, selfish and prioritising himself above everything. Unlike the "Rocky" triumph where the protagonist learns from his mistakes, Mauser doesn't develop throughout the movie. This I find amazing. The ending scene raises questions like would he have returned if allowed to play in the championship? I doubt it. Mauser hadn't changed but had to accept his circumstances or perhaps he brought them upon himself through his carelessness. Instead of a typical triumph story where the protagonist learns from mistakes and wins glory, this film presents a more realistic portrayal. Mauser represents a significant portion of the population, showing that people don't learn from mistakes. They're selfish and egocentric, willing to do anything to get what they want.

I find this movie unique and amazing in many ways. It reminds us that not everyone is trustworthy and that people don't change.

Another interesting aspect is the theme of cheating. While Mauser himself may not be cheating, all his partners are. Cheating has become normalised in films lately, and this is something to consider.

raw_payload.author: SethR-55
raw_payload.content:
This movie was absolutely remarkable, drawing me in within the first 5 minutes with already being in the world of ping pong with Marty struggling to get to London and what he's willing to do to get there and to be on top of his game in ping pong and once gets there he knows that he's the best player in the whole tournament with his cockiness and ego, flying through the first rounds like nothing and having fun along the way even with his concerns about the new Japanese players in the event, with their technique that he's never seen before. Watching how sly and cunning he is to the reporters and to Mrs Rockwell shows what kind of person he is, being able to use his words to achieve his goals. Watching the downfall of the character going from the most prideful person to someone who is willing to sacrifice everything in order to get back to the top due to him loosing in the final was incredible! Watching him go through the games people were putting him through as soon as he gets back from being a globe trotter after his defeat was really interesting and hard to watch at some points, having to endure his uncle almost having him arrested to the girl, Rachel, he got pregnant, holding him responsible for the baby all at once was intriguing and difficult to imagine what if felt to Marty having quite literally everything go wrong, for it to go even worse for him when he meets up with his buddy, Wally. Watching everything unfold with the bathtub falling through the ceiling onto the mobster's arm and dog to him loosing the dog due to him and Walter getting jumped by all the people they conned money off of was a wild experience that left me speechless. Even during the third act of the movie with the Rachel being his accomplice to get money for his debts that have caught up to him while being on top of the world was an amusement to behold with the whole, I'm getting that guys dog back to chase out even more to make the Japan trip, to him trying every way possible to get to Japan with sleeping with Mrs. Rockwell, since she's a washed up actress with a rich husband, to pleading with her Husband to get money was brilliant to the final showdown of the film with Marty beating his rival and being on the complete top of the world was just the cherry on top. And to finish off the movie to him having set his pride back in order, having completed his purpose and moving on to his next purpose in life was a great way to end this incredible film.

I would definitely recommend this film to people who want to enjoy a great story and who want to be sucked into the world. 9.8 out of 10 for me.

raw_payload.author: christopheratkinson-52249
raw_payload.content:
I'm dumbfounded by the negative reviews of this movie. It's a masterpiece.

It's a captivating, woven, and purposeful story about destiny that showcases the pitfalls of avoiding it, the dangers of living spontaneously, and the glory of its pursuit, both within a single character and through the background and progression of other characters.

The acting is tremendous, the plot reaches multiple crescendos, each that build the story and then conclude with a layered and powerfully moving message about meaning itself.

If you don't like this move, I have to assume you did not even properly absorb it.

raw_payload.author: bb52366
raw_payload.content:
From the opening title card to the end credits, Marty Supreme felt special, like one of those nostalgic films that you know your going to watch 15 years from now and still cherish. At first I thought the 80s style soundtrack/score may hinder it due to the film being set in the 1950s but it ends up working perfectly.

The cinematography is absolutely beautiful and it truly looks like it takes place in the 1950s due to the great costuming and set pieces. Every single actor that appears on screen in this film does a fantastic job but this is definitely Timothée Chalamet's movie.

There are maybe two scenes throughout the 2 hour and 30 min runtime where Chalamet is not on screen. He does a fantastic job playing the narcissistic hustler Marty Mauser and I would not be surprised if he ends up getting the golden statue for this performance. He is that good.

If you liked Uncut Gems I would highly recommend checking this film out. It has that same anxiety inducing tension and there were plenty of laugh out loud moments as well. Easily one of the best films I've seen this year.

raw_payload.author: MackT-84
raw_payload.content: This movie is essentially 1950s Good Time but the one thing that makes Good Time a stellar generational movie is stripped away in Marty Supreme, which is a main character unbound by morality, a conscious, or even a purpose. Great cameos and performances all around, even from the non actors involved, are thrown into a regurgitated Good Time-esque plot and tempo. raw_payload.author: Maverick_1332

raw_payload.author: blanbrn
raw_payload.content: I was never really a fan of tennis or table tennis for that matter. However I always enjoyed sports historical dramas, and with this bio pic "Marty Supreme" I gave it a watch and it's an epic long journey of one man's life and he fights for success and meaning and finding love thru his life. The scenes and cinematography of this film is in fine all pro form as it really captures the past and the times of yesteryear. And the performances are spot on with Timothee Chalamet being strong and tough and direct as a young determined table tennis player. Also in a good supporting turn that spices things up in the film is Gwyneth Paltrow as Mrs. Stone an elegant sexy and attractive well to do married lady that Marty Mauser beds and has an affair with. raw_payload.author: BA_Harrison
raw_payload.content:
Two and a half hours in the company of one of the most unlikeable movie characters imaginable - a liar, a cheat, a thief, and a bad sport - with a resolution that completely fails to satisfy, Josh Safdie's Marty Supreme left me wondering what the point of the whole thing was. I had hoped that the film would finally help me understand why Chalamet is a star, but I still don't get it.

Chalamet plays Marty Mauser, who wants to make it big in the world of table tennis. After disgracing himself at the world championships, he hopes to redeem himself at the next tournament in Japan, but needs to raise the money to get himself there. He involves Rachel Mizler (Odessa A'zion), the married childhood friend who he has knocked up, in a scheme to extort cash from a gangster searching for his missing dog. He also seduces famous actress Kay Stone (Gwyneth Paltrow) behind the back of her wealthy businessman husband, who Marty eventually approaches to make a deal that will get him to Japan.

Marty Supreme is a noisy, frenetic mess of a film that charges clumsily from one chaotic scene to the next, with Chalamet becoming more and more unbearable as time drags on (not that any of the characters in this film are likeable). There's lots of screaming and shouting, and the soundtrack is frequently too loud. The ending, in which Marty eventually achieves his goal of beating the world champion, lacks any meaningful impact thanks to it not being a tournament game and because Marty is still a thoroughly obnoxious person. It's impossible to give a damn. The film delivers no character arc and no sense of redemption.

My rating is 2.5/10 (for the table tennis scenes, which are well handled) rounded up to 3 for the make-up (those acne scars looked real).

raw_payload.author: bugsmm
raw_payload.content: Extremely entertaining film. Most movies "pushed" at the viewing public comprise a syrupy morass of remakes and sequels. Marty Supreme is an unapologetic expression of the art form. While there is a deliberate path for our "protagonist" throughout the film, the manic, Adderall-tinged pace of this film is curiously unhurried. The kaleidoscope of colorful characters are blended into improbable events like a Turkish rug woven with barbed wire. Some reviews here overlook a group of actors that worked together to produce an effort that resembles nothing currently available in theaters. The acting is humorous, risky, strong and engaging. Director Josh Saftie paints pictures that are intense, nostalgic and thought provoking. As for my title, this movie is straight Bebop. Wikipedia defines Bebop as "virtuosic improvisation based on a combination of harmonic structure, scales, and occasional references to the melody." The movie is laden with Oscar nominations, and I'd be a little disappointed if the Academy dismissed an amazing effort. I'll be seeing this movie twice.

raw_payload.author: ryanfitzg
raw_payload.content:
I wasn't sure how I was going to feel after the first 30 minutes felt too close to Uncut Gems with the title card scene of the sperm attaching to the egg, similar to the gem fading into a colonoscopy and the music and pace felt all too identical. Then the bathroom scene happened and everything from there felt like being in a car traveling 150mph down the highway for the entire second act, separating itself in tone and score, playing into the 50s setting.

Marty Mauser is an eccentric, talks too fast, womanizing, confident hustler and this is without question Timothee's best performance because of the difficult pace this character moves at. A lot of long action sequences really pull you in and the rest of the supporting cast do a great job of bouncing off of Marty and help peel back the layers of this man's drive and dreamlike future vision for himself.

Odessa and Gwyneth in particular were the two best performances outside of Chalamets. There's a scene where Rachel is having a hard conversation with Marty after not seeing him for months and the range she shows there really drew me into her characters feelings and relationship with this guy. Kevin O'Leary and Tyler, the Creator were also both great and funny. A lot of NY staple side characters thrown into this world, that you wouldn't find anywhere else.

Although Marty is cocky and blind to the absurdity of his own ambitions, you can't help but root for him.

DREAM BIG

🏓

raw_payload.author: ChipWhitleyJr
raw_payload.content:
🔟/🔟 Being a huge fan of Uncut Gems, as it's genuinely my favorite Adam Sandler movie, this was one of my most anticipated movies of the year. It didn't disappoint for a single second. Taking everything that worked so well with that, the high-stakes, stress inducing tension, and planting it into a deeper story/message with stronger characters. Standing above all of A24's previous efforts.

"What is this, you think I'm robbing you? If I was gonna rob you, you'd be dead. You'll be in a pool of blood."

Timothée Chalamet is magnetic as Marty Mauser and completely owns this movie. Unpredictable, controlling, and a performer hardly trying to be. He's someone who confidently walks into discomfort like it's a challenge waiting to be conquered. Fast-talking, constantly scheming, always chasing the next move without slowing down. Builds himself on believing in his own goals more than anyone else, even when he's nowhere near where he wants to be. I admire Leo's acting ability as much as anyone, but OBAA is maybe top 10 roles of his, while Chalamet turns in a decade defining performance. He deserves that Oscar without question.

"Yeah, I can sell shoes to an amputee so what? What's the other option?"

Safdie's editing keeps a burning intensity buzzing through every scene or montage. The soundtrack is incredibly strong and fits into the film's rhythm perfectly, never letting the suspense simmer for too long. Kevin O'Leary and Tyler, The Creator are surprisingly spot-on additions, far more than expected. Odessa A'zion and Gwyneth Paltrow elevate the emotional roots. This made me think Paltrow would've been a better choice for Diaz's role in Gangs of New York.

The ping pong competitive drive is a relatable aspect of it for me. Of course not a fraction of his skill, but I dabbled in High School having a class where we basically only played indoor games all period. So I've always loved playing honestly. Just recently too at PopStroke, winning against my friends while they won mini golf.

Really a must-watch in theaters. One of the year's greatest Best Picture contenders. Certainly my favorite and pick for best of the year. I don't know how much of it really happened, though I was fulfilled as it's a constant relentless ride that displays how engaging writing, intent, and performances can be when everything is clicking at once. This is my Challengers.

Update: I find it hilarious how most of the people hating on this movie giving it a crazy low score either: this is their first review or they clearly don't like being uncomfortable during a movie so it's not made for people like them. Then proceed to give the most Hallmark looking movies a 10/10. Only take into account the reviews who actually talk about the movie. The blind hate from the casuals is tiresome. Never listen to anyone who only has 1 review and it's a dedicated hate post.

#SCHWEP.

raw_payload.author: Waffles421
raw_payload.content:
I watched Safdie's earlier film Good Time leading up to this and can feel and see the parallels to this.

It's got a similar chaotic script style and appears to be the same artist on the sound score. Safdie's taste is evident and it's his best film yet.

Not in line with the marketing however, which I interpret as them marketing an inspirational pursuit of dreams, this is not an inspirational movie nor does it feel human or rooted in reality. It feels like 'Good Time'. Chaos for entertainment sake. In turn also like 'Good Time' it made me anxious and I left the theater with a lot of anxiety. I wish it was more human and relatable but that's what tends to happen with big budget thriller films. They leave you anxious and entertained. Inspiration isn't what they are offering here. I'd separate the Marty Supreme marketing campaign from the film offering, the inspiration can be found in the marketing roll out not so much in the film.

raw_payload.author: ericreimsf
raw_payload.content:
Everything that can go wrong, goes wrong.

I've read a lot of reviews praising the movie's photography, customs and so on... I will point out a few of the negatives.

1. Unnecessarily long: this movie is about 2:30 hours long and the story doesn't follow a straight line. It feells like it's 4 or 5 episodes in the life of Marty.

2. Everything that can go wrong goes wrong: "Murphy" doesn't get a single break, he finds himself im a terrible situation, that keeps getting worse and worse in a way that feels comedic.

3. What is it about really? Somehow the best ping pong player in the world, who is american, can't get the money to go to the world championship. Really?

The worse part is that he actually has the money at several points during the movie but somehow keeps losing it onmy to get it back in an even more far-fetched way.

4. Two- two-two: the movie is so long and the story is so weak that everything happens twice. The main character screws over two different friends, sleeps with two different married women, crashes two cars, gets in two fights, has two necklace incidents and plays against his nemesis... twice.

5. The best table tenis player that never trains: if you're expecting a sport's movie about resilience, effort and redemption, this is not it. Marty loses horribly against his nemesis, goes back home, gets into fights, crashes cars, steals a dog, a necklace, gets shot at (twice)... and somehow during this time he gets better at ping pong.

raw_payload.author: lukestojack

raw_payload.author: masonsaul
raw_payload.content:
Marty Supreme is one of the best sports movies ever made by delivering the expected high stakes sports drama in the first and third acts but it's enhanced further by the constant escalation in the second act where everything keeps going wrong in an almost never ending hustle, resulting in an always highly engaging 150 minute run time. It's darkly comedic and at times unbearably tense in a similar fashion to Uncut Gems before ending on an unexpectedly emotionally satisfying note.

Timothée Chalamet gives his most impressive performance to date with a real magnetism which makes it impossible to look away as he fully commits to Marty's unwavering ambition even when it only results in him sinking further. He manages to sustain the character's childlike arrogance and fittingly never ending energy throughout as well. The film also has a terrific supporting ensemble where a surprisingly good Kevin O'Leary can hold his own alongside very strong turns from Gwyneth Paltrow, Tyler, The Creator and Odessa A'zion.

Josh Safdie's direction doesn't take long to show exactly how this film will operate thanks to a hilariously bold opening titles sequence and ends things on a perfect needle drop whilst once again reuniting with a lot of his frequent collaborators. Darius Khondji's faultless cinematography handles all the chaos gracefully and captures the table tennis sequences beautifully so they are genuinely thrilling. Daniel Lopatin's electric score is a real highlight, adding even greater life to a film that already feels so alive.

raw_payload.author: cfmeyernd
raw_payload.content:
While this movie has a propulsive energy, I didn't care about any of the characters. Every character is self-absorbed and Marty is supreme in his narcissism. There is just no one worth cheering for.

Timothée is just playing a shallow character whom I imagine is like himself in his non-film life. Same with Kevin O'Leary.

I did enjoy seeing Gwyneth Paltrow in a film again, but it is entirely unconvincing how her character is seduced by Marty. I never bought the seduction.

The best feature of the film is the soundtrack , although the sounds levels range from a whisper to deafening.

If you enjoy Safdie brothers movies, then I expect you'll like Marty Supreme. If you're looking for a film that entertains and emotionally engages you, I wouldn't waste 2 1/2 hours of your time on it.

raw_payload.author: Theworldneedsmoremovies
raw_payload.content:
This movie was a last minute decision and glad I did, although I am going to go watch it again. There were a lot of distractions at the theater, which surprised me on a weekday afternoon, yet I don't want those distractions to bias my thoughts on this movie. Yet, unfortunately, movies can get affected by cinematic experience.

Yet this is definitely a cinema-oriented movie, a big screen experience, so even with external variables, I'm happy that I went.

The story immediately got my interest. The actors are interesting and good actors (a large cast with excellent chemistry, some well known, others less known, but all delivered and brought something special to their characters), the character the movie is based on is fascinating, so the story pulled me off, and there were a lot of surprise change-ups, including some "oh gross" moments. The cinematography had some impressive moments. Costumes, clothes, makeup, music all that worked.

Honestly, I said all these good things about the movie when I was annoyed: the cinema seats seemed very worn out, which is a surprise considering the venue/location (very upscale area and mall), some couples even disagreed over where to seat and had a non-verbal standoff, with one sitting in one spot and the other spot until the one gave in and went to other, people came in late and still coming in halfway through movie, someone had their cell phone on, some people were having a lower verbal chit-chat. Even with all those things, the movie still kept me there and my interest.

Rather than complain cinema about all those things(employees at ticket check and concessions were so nice too), I thought, "I've got a pass, I can catch it again." Besides, you do not always need to finish all the time, right? It builds the excitement when you do it again and get to relax and release into the whole entire movie experience.

I will update my review on the next viewing in a day or so, but go watch this movie at the cinema. These sorts of disruptions have seldom, if ever happened to me, so it was one of those things. So any movie that can hold up to those things is a good one.

raw_payload.author: rianrizvi
raw_payload.content:
Safdie gives us another dose of pure hustle.

Unlike Uncut Gems, this movie is placed in the late 40s, early 50s, which gives the viewer a truer taste of real life across the ages.

People are complaining they had a miserable time, I get why they would say that. When we expect Coca Cola and instead taste coffee, it's disgusting. Viewers have been conditioned to a sweet taste. They expect to experience life in a movie like a roller-coaster ride, on safe rails, where the bumps are somewhat predictable, and they're brought safely home to the landing. This is different. This is a truer experience, showing how raw our nerves are rubbed by life. It's still fantasy, obviously, he's a world champion, he gets the movie star, he travels the world in style, but like with the TV show, Succession, it's gives it to you with the disappointments and struggles that go with it.

At this point, most movies are boring fantasies, like the intros of success influencers. I'm bored of that garbage.

I love too how the movie ends, all you can hear at the end as the credits come on. This is life and that sound captures it well.

raw_payload.author: cinemapersonified
raw_payload.content:
Rating - 8.9: Overall, Josh Safdie shows why he is one of the best directors in Hollywood as he works with Timothée Chalamet to create a thrill ride that takes you on an anxiety-filled adventure across both NYC and the world as you follow Marty Mauser's pursuit of greatness, showing both its benefits and its sacrifices; while Josh Safdie executes this movie well on so many levels, it is held back by its middle section dragging because the side quests, while necessary, really take away from the focus of the movie.

Direction - Great: The macroscale is built similarly to 'Uncut Gems' as it is great at building this high-anxiety environment that encapsulates Marty Mauser's pursuit of greatness at all costs; The direction on a microscale is great as Safdie gets the most out of his actors to build high-intensity interpersonal scenes that move the story forward and keep you engaged; The fact that Safdie continues to get the most out of non-actors shows not only the excellence in casting but his ability to get the most out of the people he is working with; The storytelling is very good as it creates an anxiety-thrilling epic across both NYC and the world as we see Marty test his limits in pursuit of greatness; He builds tension perfectly as he creates a tonally similar movie to 'Uncut Gems' with how anxiety-inducing it is

Story - Good to Very Good: The concept is a loose biopic about a young, cocky table tennis player who is in pursuit of greatness as he goes across the world to survive and be the best possible version of himself; The plot structure is an epic as we go across both NYC and the world as Marty does whatever it takes to become the greatest ping-pong player; This epic structure works for the most part, but the side quests in the middle hour of the movie do drag down the film; The character writing for Marty Mauser is so infectious that it inspires you to be something bigger than yourself

Screenplay - Very Good: The dialogue is so fast-paced and anxiety-inducing that it fuels the adrenaline rush of the movie; The humor makes this movie funny and helps make the overall experience entertaining despite how anxiety-inducing it is; The symbolism is strong as building Marty Mauser's identity makes you leave the movie both inspired to pursue greatness while simultaneously showing you what it means to be content and have enough; The foreshadowing is strong and helps give the movie a sense of closure, which is something previous Safdie movies lacked

Acting - Very Good: Timothée Chalamet - Great (The biggest reason why this movie works because he IS Marty Supreme. The way he fights to show he is the best in his profession, and is so obsessed with greatness that he will stop at nothing to achieve his dream, is captivating; All the other performances work through him, as he is the reason everyone delivers an elevated performance in this movie), Gwyneth Paltrow - Good to Very Good (While her role is minor, she her presence is known on screen as she shows her experience as an actress and has this dynamic with Chalamet that works very well for this movie and their respective characters), Odessa A'zion - Good to Very Good (Give a bigger role and makes the most out of his as she shows range and has very good chemistry with Chalamet, helping him in this con-filled adventure), Kevin O'Leary - Very Good (basically plays his real-life persona, but it works so well because his dynamic with Chalamet makes him both a beneficiary of and an antagonist to Marty Mauser; also, it would be very funny if he started getting nominated for this performance.), Tyler Okonma - Good to Very Good (Basically plays Tyler, The Creator, but it works so well for his character as his chemistry with Chalamet really makes his scenes), Abel Ferrara - Good (Plays his character well), Fran Drescher - Good (Has good chemistry with Chalamet as she plays this mother-son dynamic well), Rest of the cast - Good (Josh Safdie continues to show why he's one of the best directors in Hollywood as he gets so much out of his actors and is able to get career best performances from non-actors; everyone in this cast works well to help tell the story and make this an anxiety-filled adventure)

Score - Very Good: The score sets the tone by creating an anxiety rush that keeps you locked into the chaos

Soundtrack - Very Good: The soundtrack is an extension of the score, helping to tell the story and set the emotions of the scene

Cinematography - Very Good: The fast cuts and different camera angles feel well-executed as they aid the fast-paced nature of the movie, helping create an adrenaline rush that keeps you on the edge of your seat; The use of film is visible, as the grain adds a sense of grit and rawness to the story

Editing - Very Good: The editing is an extension of the cinematography as its fast edits feel well-executed, aiding in the fast-paced nature of the movie and helping create an adrenaline rush that keeps you on the edge of your seat

Sound - Very Good: The sound helps pump up the anxiety and tension throughout the movie as it is a key factor that keeps your heart pumping and makes you lean to the edge of your seat

Production Design - Good: The production design helps create New York as a character as the raw grit aids Marty's pursuit of greatness; The shots outside of New York help show how Marty will go around the world to pursue his dreams, helping amplify the stakes in the climax with how foreign the world is to him

Costumes - Good: The costumes help date the movie and create some iconic outfits for Marty and the other characters

Pacing - The pacing is incredibly fast like 'Uncut Gems' to mimic the high intensity of Marty's pursuit of greatness, but the movie does feel a bit slow in the middle when it drags with these side quests

Climax - The climax is very well-executed as it feels like a pinnacle for the rush you go on with Marty as he tries to prove to both the world and himself what he is capable of doing; The ending feels satisfying and helps show the importance of being content in life

Tone - The tone is similar to 'Uncut Gems' as the high-paced, anxiety-inducing rush throughout the movie invokes feelings I felt from the last Safdie brothers film.

raw_payload.author: Paulina-5967
raw_payload.content:
I genuinely don't understand where all the negative reviews are coming from - the movie was brilliant. It explored how far arrogance can take someone and just how much it can drive a person. In a strange way, I found it motivating. Not because of the character's flaws, but because of the mindset: refusing to let anything stand in the way of what you want. There's something powerful about that.

I also loved seeing the story told through the eyes of a protagonist who isn't constantly trying to be "good." Those kinds of movies can be exhausting. It was refreshing to see a shift in who we're supposed to root for, and I appreciated that change on the big screen.

The ending wasn't predictable in the usual sense. It wasn't about winning a championship or achieving some grand, obvious victory-it was about proving himself. He gave up everything simply to show people that he was good at what he did, that it all meant something.

I thought it was fantastic. Honestly, the rest of you are overthinking it.
